name: Digital Media & Society
description: A full-credit elective course examining how digital technologies transform society, culture, democracy, economy, and individual identity, with emphasis on critical digital citizenship.

modules.title: Digital Revolution
modules.objectives: Trace the origins and development of the digital revolution; analyze how digitization transformed communication, commerce, and culture from the 1970s to the present.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.1: The Internet and Social Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to the Internet
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Digital Revolution: From Computers to Smartphones
modules.practiceNote: Create a digital revolution timeline from 1970 to present with five key turning points
modules.assignment: Research and write a 400-word historical analysis of one pivotal moment in the digital revolution (e.g., the creation of the personal computer, the invention of the Web, the launch of the iPhone, or the emergence of social media). Explain the technology, who created it, and analyze at least three specific ways it changed how people communicate or organize society.

modules.title: Internet & World Wide Web
modules.objectives: Distinguish between the Internet and the World Wide Web; explain how the Web evolved from Web 1.0 through Web 2.0 and emerging Web 3.0 concepts.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.1: The Internet and Social Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The World Wide Web
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How the Internet Works in 5 Minutes
modules.practiceNote: Compare the features of Web 1.0 vs. Web 2.0 sites using real examples
modules.assignment: Explore three websites: one that exemplifies Web 1.0 (static, one-directional information), one that exemplifies Web 2.0 (user-generated, interactive), and one that shows emerging Web 3.0 features (decentralized, AI-powered). Write a 350-word analysis explaining the differences and what each generation of the web means for user power and participation.

modules.title: Social Networks & Platforms
modules.objectives: Analyze the architecture and business models of major social media platforms; explain how platform design shapes user behavior, relationships, and social norms.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.2: Social Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Social Networks and Digital Communication
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media Platforms Designed to Maximize Engagement
modules.practiceNote: Create a comparative analysis chart of five major social media platforms by design features, business model, and communication style
modules.assignment: Choose two social media platforms with fundamentally different design philosophies (e.g., TikTok vs. LinkedIn, or Instagram vs. Reddit). Write a 400-word comparative analysis: how does each platform's architecture (algorithm, format, anonymity level, interaction model) shape what is communicated, who has power, and what social behaviors are rewarded?

modules.title: Online Identity & Privacy
modules.objectives: Analyze how people construct and manage identity in digital spaces; evaluate privacy rights and the tensions between self-presentation and data collection.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.3: Issues and Trends in the Digital Age
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Internet Privacy and Identity
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Your Digital Identity and Online Privacy Explained
modules.practiceNote: Audit your own digital footprint by searching for yourself online and reviewing your privacy settings
modules.assignment: Conduct a personal digital footprint audit: search your name and any usernames online, review the privacy settings of three apps or platforms you use, and read the key points of one platform's data collection policy. Write a 400-word report on what you found about your digital identity, what data is being collected about you, and what privacy steps you will take.

modules.title: Data & Surveillance
modules.objectives: Explain how governments and corporations collect and use personal data; evaluate the ethical dimensions of surveillance capitalism and state surveillance.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.3: Issues and Trends in the Digital Age
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Data Privacy and Digital Surveillance
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Surveillance Capitalism: Shoshana Zuboff Explained
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a privacy policy of a major tech company for what data is collected and how it is used
modules.assignment: Research Edward Snowden's 2013 NSA revelations and the Cambridge Analytica scandal (2018). Write a 400-word comparative analysis: what data was collected in each case, who collected it and why, what ethical rules were violated, and what changes in law or technology policy resulted? Conclude with your view on the acceptable limits of data surveillance.

modules.title: Algorithms & Filter Bubbles
modules.objectives: Explain how recommendation algorithms work; analyze how filter bubbles and echo chambers affect political polarization and the quality of public discourse.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.2: Social Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Algorithms and Information Filtering Online
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media Algorithms Create Filter Bubbles
modules.practiceNote: Deliberately seek out three news stories from sources outside your normal media consumption and compare the perspectives you find
modules.assignment: Spend one week deliberately diversifying your information diet: seek out news sources, social media accounts, or YouTube channels that represent perspectives different from your usual consumption. Write a 400-word reflection: what perspectives were you missing? What was uncomfortable or surprising? How did algorithmic recommendations try to pull you back to familiar content? What does this tell you about filter bubbles?

modules.title: Cyberbullying & Online Safety
modules.objectives: Define cyberbullying and online harassment; evaluate the legal, psychological, and social dimensions of digital harm and effective prevention strategies.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.3: Issues and Trends in the Digital Age
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cyberbullying and Online Safety
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Understanding Cyberbullying and How to Respond
modules.practiceNote: Develop a personal online safety plan using recommended best practices
modules.assignment: Research a documented cyberbullying case (you may use publicly reported cases). Write a 400-word case analysis: describe what happened, identify what made it worse (platform design, bystander behavior, anonymity), what harm resulted for the victim, what legal or school responses occurred, and what YOU would do if you witnessed someone being cyberbullied.

modules.title: Digital Divide & Access
modules.objectives: Analyze the dimensions of the digital divide across income, geography, age, and nation; evaluate policy approaches to expanding equitable digital access.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.3: Issues and Trends in the Digital Age
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Internet and Digital Access
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Digital Divide: Who Gets Left Behind?
modules.practiceNote: Compare internet access rates across five countries at different income levels
modules.assignment: Research internet access statistics in the United States and one developing country. Write a 400-word analysis: what are the primary barriers to access (cost, infrastructure, digital literacy), who is most affected, and what are the concrete consequences of being on the wrong side of the digital divide in education, employment, and civic participation? Propose two specific policy solutions.

modules.title: E-Commerce & Digital Economy
modules.objectives: Analyze the structure and growth of the digital economy including e-commerce, gig economy platforms, and the creator economy; evaluate the social and labor consequences of economic digitization.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.1: The Internet and Social Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How E-Commerce Changed the Economy
modules.practiceNote: Map your personal participation in the digital economy over one week (purchases, gig services, streaming subscriptions, content creation)
modules.assignment: Research how a gig economy platform (Uber, DoorDash, Fiverr, or a similar platform) works. Write a 400-word analysis examining: the business model, who benefits, what the working conditions and legal status of 'gig workers' are, and whether you believe gig economy platforms represent innovation that benefits workers and consumers, or exploitation that should be regulated. Support your position with evidence.

modules.title: AI & Automation
modules.objectives: Explain how artificial intelligence and automation are transforming work, communication, and decision-making; evaluate the ethical and social implications of AI systems.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 16.1: Globalization and the Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Overview
modules.practiceNote: Identify five specific jobs or tasks in your community that could be affected by AI automation
modules.assignment: Research how AI is currently being used in one specific industry (healthcare, journalism, criminal justice, transportation, or another sector). Write a 400-word analysis: what are AI's benefits in this industry, what are the risks or harms (bias, job displacement, accountability gaps), and what ethical safeguards should be in place to ensure AI is used responsibly?

modules.title: Digital Journalism
modules.objectives: Analyze how digital media has transformed journalism practice, business models, and the public's relationship with news; evaluate the rise of citizen journalism and misinformation.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 4.3: The Newspaper Industry Today
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Digital Journalism and the News Ecosystem
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Digital Media Changed Journalism
modules.practiceNote: Use SIFT to evaluate the credibility of three digital news sources reporting on the same story
modules.assignment: Select a major current event and find five different digital sources covering it: a legacy newspaper website, a digital-native news outlet, a social media post, a YouTube video report, and a blog or opinion site. Write a 400-word comparative analysis evaluating the credibility, accuracy, and journalistic standards evident in each source, and explain which you consider most trustworthy and why.

modules.title: Content Creation & Influencers
modules.objectives: Analyze the creator economy and influencer culture; evaluate the commercial, ethical, and cultural implications of social media content creation as a career and cultural force.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 12.1: History of Advertising
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Influencer Marketing and the Creator Economy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Creator Economy: How Influencers Are Changing Media
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a sponsored post from an influencer for transparency, persuasive techniques, and audience impact
modules.assignment: Identify a social media influencer in a field you follow (fitness, gaming, fashion, science, etc.). Write a 400-word critical analysis: how does this person make money, what is their relationship with their audience (authentic or parasocial?), how do they disclose sponsored content, what values do they promote, and what impact — positive or negative — do you believe they have on their audience's beliefs and behaviors?

modules.title: Digital Activism
modules.objectives: Evaluate how digital media tools enable and transform social movements; analyze both the strengths and limitations of online activism.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 14.2: The Media and Elections
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Digital Media and Political Participation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Social Media and Social Movements
modules.practiceNote: Analyze how a social movement used digital tools (hashtags, viral videos, crowdfunding) to organize and spread its message
modules.assignment: Research a digital-era social movement (Black Lives Matter, #MeToo, Fridays for Future, Arab Spring, etc.). Write a 400-word analysis: how did digital tools (social media, smartphones, crowdfunding) enable the movement to organize, raise awareness, and achieve goals? What were the digital strategy's limitations (online-to-offline translation, government repression, platform censorship)? Would the movement have been possible before the digital age?

modules.title: Future Digital Citizenship
modules.objectives: Apply digital citizenship principles to navigate an increasingly complex digital future; evaluate emerging technologies and develop a personal framework for responsible digital engagement.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 16.2: Media and Culture Around the World
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Digital Citizenship and Responsible Online Engagement
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What Does Good Digital Citizenship Look Like?
modules.practiceNote: Write a personal Digital Citizenship Pledge committing to specific responsible behaviors
modules.assignment: Write a 450-word Personal Digital Citizenship Statement. Drawing on what you have learned in this course, articulate: (1) four specific principles that will guide your digital behavior (truth, privacy, fairness, safety, etc.); (2) one specific change you will make to your media consumption habits; (3) how you will contribute positively to online discourse; and (4) what you believe the government and technology companies should do to create a more just and safe digital world.

quizQuestions.question: The digital revolution refers primarily to the transition from:
options: Radio to television broadcasting
Analog to digital information storage and transmission
Print to video-based media
Government-controlled to private media ownership
quizQuestions.explanation: The digital revolution is the shift from analog (continuous signal) to digital (binary code) technologies, transforming how information is created, stored, and transmitted.

quizQuestions.question: Moore's Law (Gordon Moore, 1965) predicted that computing power would:
options: Decline in cost by half every two years
Double approximately every two years while costs fell
Reach a physical limit within 20 years
Only be useful for large corporate and government applications
quizQuestions.explanation: Moore's Law observed that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years while costs fall, accurately predicting exponential growth in computing power.

quizQuestions.question: The ARPANET, developed in 1969, is historically significant as:
options: The first commercial television broadcasting network
The precursor to the modern internet, developed by the US Department of Defense
The world's first digital computer, developed at MIT
The first satellite communication system for telephone calls
quizQuestions.explanation: ARPANET (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) was a US military and academic network that became the technical foundation for the modern internet.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'digitization'?
options: The process of publishing content online for free
Converting information from analog formats to digital binary code
Teaching older generations to use digital technology
Replacing human workers with computer automation
quizQuestions.explanation: Digitization is the conversion of information (text, audio, images, video) into a binary digital format that can be processed, stored, and transmitted by computers.

quizQuestions.question: The smartphone's most transformative social impact has been:
options: Making phone calls cheaper than landlines
Putting always-connected, GPS-enabled, camera-equipped computing in every person's pocket
Eliminating the need for personal computers in homes
Making television viewing obsolete for younger generations
quizQuestions.explanation: The smartphone integrated communication, camera, GPS, and internet access into a portable device, fundamentally changing how people interact with information and each other continuously throughout the day.

quizQuestions.question: The World Wide Web (WWW) is best described as:
options: The same thing as the Internet
A system of interconnected hypertext documents accessible via the Internet
A private corporate network for business communication
A government-controlled information distribution system
quizQuestions.explanation: The Web is one application that runs on the Internet — a system of interlinked hypertext pages accessed through browsers. The Internet is the global network infrastructure; the Web is a service built on it.

quizQuestions.question: Web 2.0 differed from Web 1.0 primarily in that it:
options: Was faster due to improved server technology
Enabled user-generated content and interactive participation rather than static information delivery
Was controlled by a global regulatory body rather than individuals
Required users to pay subscription fees to access content
quizQuestions.explanation: Web 2.0 (mid-2000s) transformed the internet from a read-only medium into a participatory platform where users create, share, and interact with content through social media, wikis, and blogs.

quizQuestions.question: Tim Berners-Lee is credited with inventing:
options: The Internet (ARPANET)
The World Wide Web and the first web browser
The first social media platform
The smartphone operating system
quizQuestions.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989–1991, creating HTML, HTTP, and URL standards along with the first web browser and server.

quizQuestions.question: Net neutrality is the principle that:
options: All internet content should be politically neutral
Internet Service Providers must treat all data traffic equally without discrimination
Only government-approved websites can operate on the internet
Internet access should be free for all citizens
quizQuestions.explanation: Net neutrality requires ISPs to transmit all internet data at equal speeds and without blocking, throttling, or creating paid fast lanes for specific content or services.

quizQuestions.question: A key feature of Web 3.0 concepts is:
options: Returning to static, read-only web pages for simplicity
Decentralization, semantic web capabilities, and user ownership of data
Government regulation of all web content through licensing
Eliminating text-based content in favor of video only
quizQuestions.explanation: Web 3.0 concepts emphasize decentralization (blockchain), semantic data, AI integration, and user control of personal data, as opposed to Web 2.0's platform-centric model.

quizQuestions.question: The attention economy model of social media means that:
options: Social media users pay for content with their subscription money
Platforms profit by capturing user attention and selling it to advertisers
Users get paid for the time they spend on platforms
Attention to social media improves learning and mental focus
quizQuestions.explanation: In the attention economy, social media platforms compete to maximize time-on-site because user attention is the commodity they sell to advertisers.

quizQuestions.question: Variable reward schedules, as applied in social media design, keep users engaged by:
options: Providing consistent, predictable rewards for every interaction
Providing unpredictable rewards (likes, notifications) that create addictive checking behavior
Rewarding users only when they have spent 30+ minutes on the platform
Showing different content to different users based on age
quizQuestions.explanation: Like slot machines, social media uses variable reward schedules — unpredictable likes, comments, and notifications — to create compulsive checking behavior by activating dopamine reward pathways.

quizQuestions.question: Context collapse on social media occurs when:
options: A platform's servers fail, causing users to lose access
Content designed for one audience reaches multiple different audiences simultaneously
Users forget the context in which they originally posted content
Algorithms remove content that violates community standards
quizQuestions.explanation: Context collapse happens when content intended for a specific audience (close friends) becomes visible to other audiences (employers, strangers, family), creating communication challenges.

quizQuestions.question: Network effects in social media platforms refer to the phenomenon where:
options: Larger platforms have faster connection speeds
A platform becomes more valuable as more people use it
Networks share advertising revenue with content creators proportionally
Larger platforms pay more in government taxes
quizQuestions.explanation: Network effects mean a platform's value increases as more users join — each new user adds value for all existing users, creating a competitive moat for dominant platforms.

quizQuestions.question: Platform governance refers to:
options: The technical engineering decisions that determine platform architecture
The rules, policies, and enforcement mechanisms that determine what behavior is allowed on a platform
Government regulation of social media company stock prices
The democratic election of platform executives by users
quizQuestions.explanation: Platform governance encompasses the content moderation policies, terms of service, algorithmic decisions, and enforcement mechanisms that determine who can say what on a platform.

quizQuestions.question: Your 'digital footprint' refers to:
options: The physical environmental impact of your electronic device usage
All data traces — active and passive — generated by your online activities
The number of devices connected to your home network
Your history of online purchases over the past year
quizQuestions.explanation: Your digital footprint includes both active traces (posts, uploads) and passive traces (location data, browsing history, click behavior) left by your online activities.

quizQuestions.question: The 'right to be forgotten,' established by the European Union, allows individuals to request that:
options: Social media companies never save any of their data
Search engines remove certain personal information from search results
Former employers remove job records from public databases
Governments delete criminal records after a set period of time
quizQuestions.explanation: The EU's GDPR includes the right to erasure (right to be forgotten), allowing individuals to request deletion of personal data from search engine results under certain conditions.

quizQuestions.question: Online disinhibition describes the tendency for people to:
options: Become more reserved and careful in online communication
Express themselves more freely or recklessly online than in face-to-face contexts
Limit their social media use after experiencing negative interactions
Become better listeners when communicating through text
quizQuestions.explanation: Online disinhibition — driven by anonymity, invisibility, asynchronous communication, and reduced social cues — leads people to communicate in ways they would not in person.

quizQuestions.question: Self-presentation on social media typically involves:
options: Presenting an unfiltered, completely honest portrayal of daily life
Selectively curating a presentation of identity that emphasizes positive and aspirational aspects
Following strict community standards about what personal information can be shared
Sharing only factual, verifiable information about oneself
quizQuestions.explanation: Research shows social media users engage in strategic self-presentation — selecting, filtering, and editing their posts to project a particular identity, often emphasizing positive highlights.

quizQuestions.question: Strong passwords protect online privacy primarily by:
options: Making it difficult for your internet provider to read your emails
Making it computationally difficult for attackers to guess or crack your account access
Preventing social media companies from collecting your data
Ensuring your content is encrypted from end to end
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong, unique passwords protect accounts by making brute-force or dictionary attacks computationally infeasible, protecting personal data from unauthorized access.

quizQuestions.question: Surveillance capitalism, as described by Shoshana Zuboff, refers to:
options: Government surveillance of citizens through security cameras
A business model where companies extract and commodify user behavioral data to predict and modify future behavior
Citizens voluntarily monitoring their government through digital tools
Corporate security systems that monitor employees at work
quizQuestions.explanation: Zuboff's surveillance capitalism describes how Google, Facebook, and others harvest behavioral data, use it to build predictive models, and sell these predictions to advertisers seeking to influence behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The NSA surveillance programs revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013 demonstrated:
options: That the US government had no interest in digital surveillance
That government agencies were conducting mass collection of digital communications data without public knowledge
That digital encryption makes government surveillance impossible
That only foreign nationals were subject to US digital surveillance
quizQuestions.explanation: Snowden's revelations showed that the NSA was conducting mass surveillance of phone records, internet communications, and digital data — including of US citizens — without public knowledge or meaningful oversight.

quizQuestions.question: The EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requires companies to:
options: Store all user data on European servers only
Obtain informed consent before collecting personal data and give users control over their data
Share all collected user data with European government agencies
Hire only European citizens to handle user data
quizQuestions.explanation: GDPR established that EU residents must give explicit, informed consent for data collection and have rights to access, correct, and delete their personal data held by companies.

quizQuestions.question: Metadata surveillance involves collecting:
options: The actual content of messages and emails
Data about communications — who contacted whom, when, and from where — rather than content
Only publicly available social media posts
Financial transaction records from banks
quizQuestions.explanation: Metadata (data about data) — who called whom, when, how long, from what location — can reveal patterns of association and behavior even without accessing message content.

quizQuestions.question: The Cambridge Analytica scandal (2018) involved:
options: A British university's academic misconduct in a social science study
The unauthorized harvesting of Facebook user data to build political targeting profiles for election campaigns
A cyberattack on the UK government's communications infrastructure
Facebook employees leaking user passwords to third parties
quizQuestions.explanation: Cambridge Analytica harvested data from up to 87 million Facebook users without their knowledge through a personality quiz app, using the data to build psychological profiles for political advertising.

quizQuestions.question: A recommendation algorithm's primary goal is to:
options: Show users the most recent content in chronological order
Maximize user engagement by predicting and serving content they are most likely to interact with
Show users content that is most accurate and trustworthy
Distribute content equally among all creators on the platform
quizQuestions.explanation: Recommendation algorithms are optimized for engagement (clicks, watch time, shares) rather than accuracy or quality — showing content that matches past behavior to maximize time on platform.

quizQuestions.question: Eli Pariser coined the term 'filter bubble' to describe:
options: Government censorship systems that filter out political dissent
Algorithm-created information environments where users see primarily content matching their existing preferences
Social media content moderation systems that remove harmful content
Email spam filters that block unwanted commercial messages
quizQuestions.explanation: Pariser coined 'filter bubble' to describe how personalization algorithms create individual information environments where users see primarily content that confirms their preferences, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives.

quizQuestions.question: Echo chambers online are most likely to contribute to:
options: Greater understanding of diverse political perspectives
Political polarization as individuals interact primarily with like-minded people
More accurate information consumption as community members fact-check each other
Reduced political participation as users become disillusioned
quizQuestions.explanation: Echo chambers reinforce existing beliefs by surrounding users primarily with voices that agree, reducing exposure to challenges and potentially intensifying political views toward more extreme positions.

quizQuestions.question: Algorithmic amplification refers to the way recommendation systems:
options: Slow down the distribution of content that violates community standards
Preferentially distribute emotionally engaging content, including outrage-inducing content, at higher rates
Guarantee that all content reaches equal numbers of followers
Share content only with users who have specifically opted in
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic amplification occurs when systems systematically boost reach of highly engaging content — including outrage-inducing or false content — because it generates more reactions and therefore more time-on-site.

quizQuestions.question: The most effective individual strategy for breaking out of a filter bubble is:
options: Deleting all social media accounts permanently
Deliberately seeking out credible sources presenting perspectives different from your usual consumption
Only following accounts with large follower counts as proxy for reliability
Relying on television news instead of social media
quizQuestions.explanation: Deliberately diversifying information sources — seeking out credible outlets with different editorial perspectives — is the most practical strategy for countering algorithmic filter bubble effects.

quizQuestions.question: Cyberbullying differs from traditional bullying primarily because it:
options: Only occurs between minors under the age of 18
Can reach victims anywhere 24/7 and can persist permanently through screenshots and sharing
Is less harmful because it doesn't involve physical contact
Can only be investigated by law enforcement, not schools
quizQuestions.explanation: Cyberbullying's distinguishing features include its 24/7 reach into victims' homes, the potential permanence of digital content, and the rapid amplification through sharing.

quizQuestions.question: The bystander effect in online harassment refers to the tendency of witnesses to:
options: Always intervene immediately when they see harassment online
Fail to intervene because they assume someone else will act
Report all harassment to platform moderators automatically
Leave the platform rather than witness ongoing harassment
quizQuestions.explanation: The online bystander effect occurs when witnesses to harassment fail to act — diffusion of responsibility in large online audiences means each person assumes others will report or respond.

quizQuestions.question: Doxxing refers to the online practice of:
options: Creating false documentation about a public figure
Publicly releasing someone's private personal information (address, phone number, employer) without consent
Filing false legal documents targeting an online adversary
Documenting one's own online activities in a public blog
quizQuestions.explanation: Doxxing involves researching and publicly publishing private information about a person (real name, home address, workplace) to expose them to harassment, threats, or harm.

quizQuestions.question: The most appropriate first step when experiencing cyberbullying is:
options: Immediately responding to the bully online to defend yourself
Documenting evidence, reporting to the platform, and telling a trusted adult
Deleting your social media accounts to escape the situation
Identifying the bully's personal information and sharing it publicly
quizQuestions.explanation: The recommended response to cyberbullying includes preserving evidence (screenshots), using platform reporting tools, and involving a trusted adult or appropriate authority.

quizQuestions.question: Online radicalization refers to the process by which:
options: Young people become more politically engaged through social media
Individuals are progressively exposed to increasingly extreme content through algorithmic recommendation
News organizations adopt more sensationalized reporting styles online
Social media platforms promote radical transparency in corporate governance
quizQuestions.explanation: Online radicalization occurs when recommendation algorithms progressively serve users more extreme content — from moderate to increasingly radical viewpoints — as engagement-maximizing systems respond to viewing behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The digital divide refers to:
options: The conflict between tech companies and traditional media industries
Unequal access to digital technology across income, geographic, age, and national lines
The difference between free and paid internet content
The technical distinction between analog and digital communication systems
quizQuestions.explanation: The digital divide encompasses inequalities in access to internet, devices, digital literacy, and digital skills across different socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic groups.

quizQuestions.question: Rural communities in the US are most commonly on the wrong side of the digital divide due to:
options: Lower interest in digital technology among rural populations
Lack of broadband infrastructure investment in areas with lower population density and profitability
Government policies that prohibit internet access in rural areas
Cultural values that discourage the use of digital technology
quizQuestions.explanation: Rural areas often lack broadband because building infrastructure for dispersed, low-density populations is less profitable for private ISPs, creating persistent geographic access gaps.

quizQuestions.question: Digital literacy refers to:
options: The ability to type quickly and accurately on a keyboard
The skills to find, evaluate, create, and communicate information using digital technologies
Knowledge of programming languages and software development
The ability to read text displayed on digital screens
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital literacy encompasses a broad range of skills for effectively, safely, and critically engaging with digital tools and information — from finding credible sources to creating digital content.

quizQuestions.question: The most significant consequence of the digital divide is that those without access:
options: Develop stronger face-to-face communication skills
Are excluded from educational, economic, and civic opportunities increasingly delivered through digital channels
Are more likely to read physical books and newspapers
Have stronger privacy protections than those who use digital devices
quizQuestions.explanation: The digital divide creates inequality in access to education (online learning), economic opportunities (job applications, remote work), healthcare (telemedicine), and civic participation (government services, voting information).

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a policy approach to reducing the digital divide?
options: Reducing government spending on telecommunications infrastructure
Subsidized broadband access programs, community digital literacy training, and device lending programs
Requiring all internet content to be in one official national language
Allowing major tech companies to self-regulate internet access pricing
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective digital divide reduction strategies include government subsidies for broadband access (like the FCC's Affordable Connectivity Program), community digital literacy training, and programs providing devices to low-income families.

quizQuestions.question: E-commerce has most significantly disrupted which traditional industry?
options: Healthcare and medical services
Retail — brick-and-mortar stores have declined as online shopping grew
Banking and financial services
Entertainment and media distribution
quizQuestions.explanation: E-commerce (led by Amazon, Shopify) has caused widespread closures of physical retail stores — the 'retail apocalypse' — as consumers increasingly prefer online shopping.

quizQuestions.question: Gig economy workers (Uber drivers, DoorDash couriers, Fiverr freelancers) typically lack:
options: The ability to choose their own working hours
Traditional employment benefits like health insurance, paid leave, and unemployment insurance
Access to digital tools needed to perform their work
The legal right to set their own service prices
quizQuestions.explanation: Gig workers are typically classified as independent contractors, which means they are ineligible for employer-provided benefits (health insurance, paid time off, workers' compensation) available to employees.

quizQuestions.question: The platform economy refers to businesses that:
options: Manufacture physical products sold through retail stores
Create digital platforms that facilitate transactions between producers and consumers without owning the product
Operate public transportation platforms like subways and buses
Develop software platforms for business enterprise management
quizQuestions.explanation: Platform economy companies (Airbnb, Uber, Etsy) create digital marketplaces that connect buyers and sellers without owning the assets being transacted — they profit from facilitating exchanges.

quizQuestions.question: The creator economy enables individuals to:
options: Work as anonymous employees for large digital media corporations
Generate income directly from audiences through content creation, bypassing traditional intermediaries
Create digital products that are automatically protected by patent law
Own shares in the social media platforms they use
quizQuestions.explanation: The creator economy enables YouTubers, podcasters, Substack writers, and other content creators to monetize audiences directly through advertising revenue, subscriptions, and merchandise.

quizQuestions.question: Algorithmic wage discrimination in the gig economy refers to platforms that:
options: Pay workers different wages based on their formal educational credentials
Use algorithms to optimize pricing in ways that may unfairly reduce worker earnings
Automate the payment of wages to eliminate human error in payroll
Charge different rates based on customer credit scores
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic management in gig platforms uses data to optimize surge pricing and task assignment in ways that can systematically reduce worker earnings without their knowledge or recourse.

quizQuestions.question: Machine learning is distinguished from traditional programming in that machine learning systems:
options: Follow explicit rules programmed by human engineers
Learn patterns from data and improve performance without being explicitly programmed
Can only perform tasks that humans have previously demonstrated to them
Require constant human supervision during all operations
quizQuestions.explanation: Machine learning systems develop their own rules and improve through exposure to data, rather than following explicit human-programmed instructions for every situation.

quizQuestions.question: Algorithmic bias in AI systems occurs when:
options: AI systems are programmed to favor one company's products over competitors
AI systems reproduce or amplify biases present in the training data
AI systems work too slowly to be useful in real-world applications
AI systems are accessible to some users but not others
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic bias results from AI systems trained on historical data that reflects existing social biases — causing the AI to make discriminatory decisions in hiring, lending, policing, and other applications.

quizQuestions.question: The most significant labor market concern about AI and automation is:
options: That AI will make workers more productive and therefore create more jobs
That automation will displace large numbers of workers from routine cognitive and physical tasks
That AI tools are too expensive for most workers to access
That AI will reduce the quality of products and services consumers receive
quizQuestions.explanation: Economists and researchers project that AI and automation will significantly displace workers in routine tasks (data entry, driving, basic analysis), potentially disrupting labor markets at large scale.

quizQuestions.question: Deepfakes represent a significant threat to trust because:
options: They are created by foreign governments to undermine democracy
AI can now generate realistic synthetic video and audio that is difficult to distinguish from authentic recordings
They are used primarily to create entertainment parody content
Deep learning requires large amounts of data that are difficult for bad actors to obtain
quizQuestions.explanation: Deepfakes created by generative AI can realistically simulate people saying or doing things they never did, undermining the reliability of visual and audio evidence.

quizQuestions.question: AI accountability refers to the need for AI systems to be:
options: Profitable for the companies that develop them
Transparent, auditable, and subject to human oversight — especially when making consequential decisions
Available only to governments and regulatory bodies
Developed exclusively by academic researchers rather than corporations
quizQuestions.explanation: AI accountability requires that AI systems making consequential decisions (criminal sentencing, hiring, credit) be transparent in how they work, auditable for bias, and subject to meaningful human oversight.

quizQuestions.question: The most significant economic challenge facing digital journalism is:
options: Too many journalists competing for the same limited number of stories
The migration of advertising revenue to Google and Facebook, decimating traditional news business models
Government regulations that make it too expensive to operate news websites
Readers refusing to pay for any form of online journalism
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital advertising platforms (Google, Facebook) have captured the majority of online advertising revenue that once funded journalism, contributing to widespread newsroom layoffs and closures.

quizQuestions.question: Citizen journalism refers to news content produced by:
options: Professional journalists employed by nonprofit civic organizations
Non-professional members of the public who witness and report events
Government-funded news organizations with public service mandates
Journalists who focus exclusively on local community issues
quizQuestions.explanation: Citizen journalism involves non-professionals — regular citizens using smartphones and social media — documenting and reporting events as eyewitnesses.

quizQuestions.question: The paywalled subscription model in digital journalism addresses the business problem of:
options: Paying journalists more than print newspapers paid them
Generating revenue directly from readers rather than relying on advertising
Complying with government regulations on digital content
Limiting the reach of journalism to only high-income, educated audiences
quizQuestions.explanation: Subscription paywalls allow digital news outlets to generate direct reader revenue, reducing dependence on volatile digital advertising markets dominated by Google and Facebook.

quizQuestions.question: The SIFT method for evaluating online news is most effective when applied:
options: Only to social media posts, not established news websites
Before sharing or forming opinions on any piece of online content, regardless of apparent credibility
Only to content from sources the reader is unfamiliar with
After sharing content, as a way to verify what was shared
quizQuestions.explanation: SIFT should be applied to all online content — even from familiar sources — before forming conclusions or sharing, as misinformation appears on established-looking sites.

quizQuestions.question: News deserts are communities that have lost which specific type of journalism?
options: International and foreign affairs coverage
Local news coverage of government, schools, and community affairs
Entertainment and celebrity news coverage
Investigative journalism on national political scandals
quizQuestions.explanation: News deserts lack local journalism — reporters covering city council meetings, school boards, local courts, and community events — leaving residents without information to hold local institutions accountable.

quizQuestions.question: The FTC requires social media influencers who post sponsored content to:
options: Limit sponsored posts to once per week
Clearly and conspicuously disclose when they are being paid to promote a product
Submit all sponsored content for FTC review before posting
Avoid making specific claims about product performance
quizQuestions.explanation: FTC guidelines mandate that influencers disclose material connections (payment, free products, other compensation) clearly and conspicuously so followers know they are viewing advertising.

quizQuestions.question: Parasocial relationships with influencers are characterized by:
options: Mutual friendships formed through direct communication with influencers
One-sided emotional bonds where followers feel they know the influencer who does not know them
Formal contractual relationships between influencers and their most dedicated followers
Parasitic behavior in which influencers exploit their followers financially
quizQuestions.explanation: Parasocial relationships are one-sided connections — followers develop emotional bonds with influencers who perform intimacy and authenticity but don't know the individual follower.

quizQuestions.question: Authenticity in influencer content refers to the perception that:
options: The influencer has no commercial relationships of any kind
The influencer's content reflects genuine personality and values rather than purely commercial motives
The influencer personally creates every piece of content without assistance
The influencer has never edited or filtered any image or video
quizQuestions.explanation: Authenticity in influencer marketing is the audience perception that the influencer is being genuine and that their content reflects real values and personality rather than purely commercial calculation.

quizQuestions.question: Multi-channel networks (MCNs) in the creator economy function primarily as:
options: Government agencies regulating online content creation
Companies that manage, support, and monetize groups of creators in exchange for a share of revenue
Non-profit organizations that fund independent digital journalism
Consumer groups that represent the interests of social media users
quizQuestions.explanation: MCNs (like Maker Studios, Fullscreen) are companies that aggregate YouTube and social media channels, providing creators with resources, brand deals, and management in exchange for revenue percentages.

quizQuestions.question: The 'wellness' or 'beauty' influencer industry has attracted criticism for:
options: Promoting excessive consumption of luxury goods
Making misleading health and beauty claims, selling unregulated supplements, and promoting unrealistic body standards
Refusing to disclose sponsored relationships to their audiences
Creating content that is too sophisticated for young audiences to understand
quizQuestions.explanation: Wellness and beauty influencers have been widely criticized for promoting pseudoscientific health claims, selling unregulated supplements, and perpetuating harmful, unrealistic beauty standards.

quizQuestions.question: Hashtag activism, like #MeToo or #BlackLivesMatter, has been most effective at:
options: Directly changing legislation in the short term
Rapidly building awareness, solidarity, and a shared narrative around an issue
Replacing traditional organizing and advocacy entirely
Generating funding for social movements through crowdsourcing
quizQuestions.explanation: Hashtag campaigns excel at rapidly aggregating individual experiences, building collective identity, and raising issue salience across large networks — making them powerful tools for awareness and solidarity.

quizQuestions.question: Slacktivism refers to low-effort online participation in social causes that:
options: Achieves significant policy change through mass participation
Provides a sense of participation but may not translate into meaningful real-world action
Is exclusively practiced by younger generations who lack civic knowledge
Is the primary fundraising strategy for major nonprofits
quizQuestions.explanation: Slacktivism describes minimal effort online actions (likes, shares, profile picture filters) that provide psychological satisfaction of participation without requiring substantive commitment.

quizQuestions.question: The Arab Spring (2010–2012) demonstrated digital media's ability to:
options: Directly program the political views of massive populations
Enable rapid horizontal communication, organization, and international visibility for protest movements
Replace the need for physical protest with purely online activism
Guarantee the success of democratic movements against authoritarian regimes
quizQuestions.explanation: The Arab Spring showed how social media (Twitter, Facebook) enabled activists to coordinate across borders, communicate despite media blackouts, and gain international attention for pro-democracy movements.

quizQuestions.question: Authoritarian governments most commonly respond to digital activism by:
options: Accepting democratic demands communicated through social media
Internet shutdowns, social media censorship, doxxing and arresting activists, and spreading counter-narratives
Allowing free digital expression as a safety valve for dissent
Creating competing social media platforms with more attractive features
quizQuestions.explanation: Authoritarian states employ internet shutdowns, platform censorship, surveillance to identify and arrest activists, and state-sponsored disinformation to suppress digital dissent.

quizQuestions.question: The primary limitation of purely digital activism is:
options: That young people lack the technical skills to use digital organizing tools
The difficulty of converting online engagement into sustained offline organizing and concrete policy change
That social media platforms ban political organizing content
That digital activism is more expensive than traditional grassroots organizing
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital activism faces the challenge of translating online engagement (likes, shares, petitions) into the sustained real-world organizing, coalition building, and institutional engagement needed for lasting change.

quizQuestions.question: The Nine Elements of Digital Citizenship include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Digital access and digital literacy
Digital commerce and digital communication
Digital anonymity and complete privacy
Digital law and digital rights and responsibilities
quizQuestions.explanation: Mike Ribble's nine elements of digital citizenship include access, commerce, communication, literacy, etiquette, law, rights/responsibilities, health/wellness, and security — not 'complete anonymity.'

quizQuestions.question: Responsible sharing of information online requires which of the following habits?
options: Sharing quickly to ensure important truths spread faster than misinformation
Verifying accuracy through credible sources before sharing any piece of information
Only sharing content from accounts with verified blue checkmarks
Sharing only content that does not challenge any existing social norms
quizQuestions.explanation: Responsible information sharing requires verifying accuracy through credible, independent sources before sharing — regardless of how emotionally compelling or credible-seeming a piece of content appears.

quizQuestions.question: Digital wellness refers to:
options: Using only apps that have been medically certified as safe
Maintaining a healthy, intentional relationship with technology that supports rather than undermines wellbeing
Avoiding all digital media to protect mental and physical health
Using technology exclusively for educational and professional purposes
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital wellness is the practice of using technology intentionally and mindfully in ways that serve rather than undermine personal wellbeing, relationships, and productivity.

quizQuestions.question: Which technology policy approach would BEST protect individuals from AI-based discrimination?
options: Banning all AI systems from making decisions about people
Requiring algorithmic audits for bias, transparency about AI decision-making, and avenues for human appeal
Allowing companies to self-regulate their AI systems without government oversight
Only permitting AI in entertainment applications, not in consequential decisions
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic accountability frameworks — requiring bias audits, transparency about how AI decisions are made, and meaningful human review — are the most practical and proportionate approach to preventing AI discrimination.

quizQuestions.question: A responsible digital citizen contributes to the online information environment primarily by:
options: Creating as much content as possible to ensure diverse voices are heard
Practicing verification before sharing, engaging respectfully, and supporting quality journalism
Avoiding all political discussion online to prevent contributing to polarization
Relying only on AI tools to filter information for accuracy
quizQuestions.explanation: Responsible digital citizenship involves verification habits, respectful engagement even with disagreement, critical evaluation of sources, and support for quality journalism — contributing to a healthier information ecosystem.

questions.question: The ARPANET, developed in 1969, was historically significant because it was:
options: The first commercial TV broadcasting network
The precursor to the modern Internet, developed by US military and academic researchers
The first digital computer designed for personal home use
The original satellite communication system for international calls
questions.explanation: ARPANET was a US Defense Department-funded packet-switching network that became the direct technical foundation for the modern internet.

questions.question: The key innovation of Web 2.0 that distinguished it from Web 1.0 was:
options: Faster internet connection speeds
User-generated content and interactive, two-way participation
The introduction of email communication
Government licensing of all websites
questions.explanation: Web 2.0 transformed the internet from a one-way information delivery system into a participatory platform where users create, share, and interact with content.

questions.question: Network effects in social media mean that:
options: Platforms with better technology always attract more users
A platform becomes more valuable as more people join it
Smaller platforms provide better quality experiences than larger ones
Users share network access costs equally among all participants
questions.explanation: Network effects mean each additional user adds value for all existing users, explaining why dominant platforms (Facebook, Instagram) are hard for competitors to displace.

questions.question: Your digital footprint refers to:
options: The carbon footprint of your internet usage
All data traces generated by your online activities, both active and passive
Your number of followers and connections on social media
Your personal security credentials for online accounts
questions.explanation: Your digital footprint is the cumulative record of data generated by your online activity — both what you deliberately post and passive data like location, browsing, and click behavior.

questions.question: Surveillance capitalism, as described by Shoshana Zuboff, is an economic model where:
options: Governments pay tech companies to monitor citizens on their behalf
Tech companies extract and sell user behavioral data to predict and influence future behavior
Citizens pay for secure, ad-free internet access through subscriptions
Companies monitor their employees' digital activities for productivity
questions.explanation: Zuboff describes surveillance capitalism as a new economic logic where companies harvest behavioral data from users without meaningful consent and sell predictions about behavior to advertisers.

questions.question: Filter bubbles are created primarily by:
options: Individual users who deliberately avoid content they disagree with
Recommendation algorithms that serve users content matching their past behavior
Platform content moderation removing opposing viewpoints
Government regulations restricting certain types of political speech
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles are an algorithmic phenomenon — personalization systems that optimize for engagement create information environments where users primarily see content matching their existing preferences.

questions.question: Cyberbullying differs most significantly from traditional bullying in that it:
options: Is legally prosecuted more aggressively than physical bullying
Can follow victims anywhere and persist permanently through digital records
Only affects adolescents and not adults
Is less emotionally harmful because it lacks physical confrontation
questions.explanation: Cyberbullying's 24/7 reach (into homes and private spaces), permanence of digital evidence, and potential for viral amplification make it distinctly more pervasive than traditional bullying.

questions.question: The digital divide is most accurately described as inequalities in access to:
options: Digital entertainment and gaming technology
Internet connectivity, devices, and digital skills across socioeconomic and geographic lines
Exclusive digital content from premium creators
Cybersecurity protection for personal data
questions.explanation: The digital divide encompasses gaps in internet access, device availability, connection speed, and digital literacy skills that fall along income, geographic, age, and national lines.

questions.question: Gig economy workers are typically classified as independent contractors, which means they:
options: Have more job security than traditional employees
Lack employee benefits like health insurance, paid leave, and unemployment insurance
Are paid higher hourly wages than traditional workers in comparable roles
Have the right to collectively bargain through labor unions
questions.explanation: Independent contractor classification means gig workers bear the cost of their own benefits, taxes, and equipment — they do not receive the protections and benefits of employee status.

questions.question: Algorithmic bias in AI systems most commonly results from:
options: AI engineers deliberately programming discriminatory rules
AI systems trained on historical data that reflects existing social biases
AI systems that are too simple to make nuanced distinctions
Government regulations that restrict AI from collecting certain types of data
questions.explanation: When AI systems are trained on historical data reflecting past discrimination (in hiring, lending, policing), they learn and reproduce those biases in their predictions and decisions.

questions.question: The Cambridge Analytica scandal primarily involved:
options: Hackers stealing credit card data from Facebook users
Unauthorized harvesting of Facebook user data to build political personality profiles for targeted campaign ads
Facebook employees leaking user data to journalists
Cambridge University researchers publishing confidential survey data
questions.explanation: Cambridge Analytica obtained data from up to 87 million Facebook users through a personality quiz app and used it to build psychological profiles for targeted political advertising.

questions.question: Net neutrality requires that Internet Service Providers:
options: Make all internet content free for consumers
Treat all internet traffic equally without creating fast or slow lanes
Filter out harmful content before it reaches end users
Prioritize educational content over entertainment streaming
questions.explanation: Net neutrality prevents ISPs from discriminating between internet traffic — they cannot slow streaming services to benefit their own products or charge websites for faster delivery to customers.

questions.question: Doxxing is most harmful because it:
options: Exposes false information about targets to embarrass them
Exposes private information that enables real-world harassment, threats, and physical danger
Shares embarrassing but publicly available social media posts
Copies and republishes someone's creative work without attribution
questions.explanation: Doxxing's particular danger is that publishing real-world contact information (home address, employer, family members) facilitates harassment, stalking, and physical threats against targets.

questions.question: The creator economy enables individuals to:
options: Work anonymously as content moderators for large platforms
Generate income directly from audiences through content creation without traditional institutional intermediaries
Co-own shares in social media platforms through user investment programs
Create legally protected intellectual property at no cost through platform tools
questions.explanation: The creator economy allows individuals to monetize content directly through advertising revenue sharing, subscriptions, merchandise, and brand deals — bypassing traditional media gatekeepers.

questions.question: Variable reward schedules in social media design are similar to slot machines in that they:
options: Always deliver predictable rewards for specific behaviors
Deliver unpredictable rewards that create compulsive checking behavior by activating dopamine reward pathways
Reward users who spend the most money on the platform
Only reward verified content creators, not ordinary users
questions.explanation: Social media uses variable reward schedules — unpredictable likes, followers, comments — that trigger dopamine responses and compulsive use in the same way slot machine random rewards do.

questions.question: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989–1991 for the original purpose of:
options: Creating a commercial marketplace for global e-commerce
Allowing scientists at CERN to share research information through linked documents
Building a social networking system for university students
Providing a global telecommunications system for government use
questions.explanation: Berners-Lee invented the Web at CERN as a tool for sharing scientific research through hyperlinked documents — a far cry from its ultimate commercial and social applications.

questions.question: Context collapse on social media is best addressed by:
options: Deleting accounts on all platforms with mixed audiences
Using privacy settings intentionally and being aware of who might see any given post
Only posting content that would be appropriate for the most conservative possible audience
Using separate anonymous accounts for different aspects of life
questions.explanation: Thoughtful use of privacy settings — limiting who can see specific posts — and awareness of diverse potential audiences are practical strategies for managing context collapse.

questions.question: The EU's GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) gives individuals the right to:
options: Sue any company that shows them targeted advertising
Access, correct, and request deletion of their personal data held by companies
Use any company's digital services without accepting any terms of service
Receive monetary compensation from any company that collects their data
questions.explanation: GDPR grants EU residents rights to access data companies hold about them, correct inaccuracies, and request deletion — giving individuals meaningful control over their personal data.

questions.question: Algorithmic radicalization on social media occurs when recommendation systems:
options: Deliberately expose users to all political perspectives to create balance
Progressively serve users more extreme content as engagement-optimizing algorithms respond to viewing behavior
Remove moderate content to make room for more popular extreme content
Track users who share political content and flag their accounts for review
questions.explanation: Radicalization pathways develop when engagement-optimizing algorithms respond to a user's interest in politically charged content by serving progressively more extreme versions of that content.

questions.question: Rural communities' most significant digital divide challenge is:
options: Cultural resistance to adopting digital technology
Lack of affordable high-speed internet infrastructure due to low profitability of rural deployment
Government restrictions on rural internet access
Higher rates of device theft in rural areas
questions.explanation: Private ISPs face lower returns deploying broadband in sparsely populated rural areas, leading to chronic underinvestment and the persistence of rural connectivity gaps.

questions.question: The gig economy's labor classification debate centers on whether gig workers should be:
options: Paid at higher rates than employees for equivalent work
Reclassified as employees to receive standard employment benefits and protections
Required to form cooperative businesses rather than working for platforms
Paid by platforms rather than customers who use the service
questions.explanation: Labor advocates argue gig workers who are economically dependent on platforms should receive employee protections and benefits (health insurance, overtime, unemployment), while platforms argue independent contractor status enables flexibility.

questions.question: Machine learning systems can reproduce social biases because they:
options: Are programmed by biased engineers to favor certain groups
Learn from historical data that reflects past discriminatory practices and structures
Are not sophisticated enough to account for social differences
Deliberately amplify biases to make their decision-making more transparent
questions.explanation: When training data reflects historical biases (hiring data showing preferences for certain demographics, biased crime statistics), ML systems learn to replicate those patterns in future predictions.

questions.question: The primary business challenge facing digital journalism is:
options: Too few journalists wanting to cover local and investigative stories
Loss of advertising revenue to digital platforms (Google, Facebook) that aggregated audiences away from news
Government regulations that limit what journalists can report online
Reader disinterest in paying for any form of news content
questions.explanation: Digital advertising revenue that once supported newsrooms has migrated primarily to Google and Facebook, forcing many news organizations to cut staff, merge, or close — the news business model crisis.

questions.question: FTC requirements for influencer disclosure protect consumers by:
options: Limiting how many sponsored posts an influencer can make per month
Ensuring consumers know when content is paid advertising rather than genuine personal recommendation
Requiring influencers to guarantee the quality of products they promote
Setting maximum prices influencers can charge for sponsored posts
questions.explanation: FTC disclosure rules prevent deceptive advertising by requiring influencers to make their paid commercial relationships visible to audiences who might otherwise trust recommendations as organic.

questions.question: Hashtag activism (#MeToo, #BlackLivesMatter) has been most effective at:
options: Directly achieving legislative change without additional organizing
Rapidly building collective awareness, shared narrative, and solidarity around an issue at massive scale
Replacing all forms of traditional grassroots community organizing
Raising funds that social movements use for operational expenses
questions.explanation: Hashtag campaigns build collective consciousness rapidly by aggregating personal stories, making shared experiences visible, and creating a common vocabulary for movements.

questions.question: Responsible digital citizenship in terms of information sharing requires:
options: Sharing as quickly as possible so good information reaches people before misinformation
Verifying accuracy through credible sources before sharing any piece of information
Only sharing content from accounts that have been verified by the platform
Sharing all information under an anonymous identity to protect privacy
questions.explanation: Responsible information sharing requires stopping to verify claims through credible, independent sources before amplifying them — the most direct way individuals can reduce misinformation spread.

questions.question: AI accountability frameworks are most important for AI systems that:
options: Generate creative content like music and art
Make consequential decisions affecting people's lives such as hiring, lending, and criminal sentencing
Control entertainment recommendation systems
Optimize the efficiency of internal business processes
questions.explanation: AI accountability — transparency, bias auditing, human oversight — is most critical for systems that make decisions with major impacts on people's lives, where errors or bias cause serious harm.

questions.question: The primary limitation of online activism (slacktivism) is that:
options: Algorithms prevent political content from reaching large audiences
Low-effort online actions rarely translate into the sustained real-world organizing needed for lasting change
Young people lack the digital skills to organize effectively online
Governments quickly suppress digital activism before it can gain momentum
questions.explanation: Slacktivism describes the gap between online engagement (likes, shares, signing petitions) and the sustained real-world organizing, coalition building, and institutional engagement that typically drives policy change.

questions.question: Digital wellness involves using technology in ways that:
options: Maximize the amount of time spent consuming educational content
Support personal wellbeing, relationships, and productivity rather than undermining them
Eliminate all recreational or entertainment use of digital media
Use only platforms that have passed government health and safety reviews
questions.explanation: Digital wellness is about intentional, mindful engagement with technology — using it in ways that genuinely serve your goals and wellbeing, setting limits where technology use becomes harmful.

questions.question: Deepfakes created by generative AI represent a specific threat to:
options: The profitability of the entertainment industry
The reliability of video and audio evidence as a foundation for truth and accountability
The employment of professional actors and voice actors
Copyright protections for original creative works
questions.explanation: When AI can generate convincing synthetic video of anyone saying or doing anything, the reliability of all video evidence as proof of truth is fundamentally undermined.

questions.question: Which approach BEST addresses algorithmic amplification of misinformation?
options: Banning all political discussion from social media platforms
Redesigning algorithms to demote demonstrably false content and prioritize credible sources
Requiring users to pay a fee before sharing any content online
Having government officials review all content before it is amplified
questions.explanation: Demoting demonstrably false content and prioritizing credible sources in recommendation systems addresses the root mechanism of algorithmic misinformation amplification while preserving free expression.

questions.question: The platform economy (Airbnb, Uber) benefits consumers primarily by:
options: Providing workers with better employment conditions than traditional jobs
Creating more efficient markets by reducing transaction costs and expanding supply of services
Eliminating the need for government safety regulations in service industries
Generating tax revenue for local governments through corporate income taxes
questions.explanation: Platform markets create efficiency by matching supply and demand more effectively, often at lower prices (Uber vs. taxis, Airbnb vs. hotels) — though often at the cost of worker protections and regulatory compliance.

questions.question: Parasocial relationships with social media influencers are concerning when followers:
options: Enjoy influencer content as entertainment without deeper investment
Make significant health, financial, or personal decisions based on the influencer's unqualified advice
Engage with influencer content on multiple platforms
Follow influencers from countries other than their own
questions.explanation: Parasocial bonds can be exploited or become harmful when followers trust influencers as authoritative advisors for major decisions in health, finance, or personal relationships without adequate expertise.

questions.question: The Arab Spring demonstrated that social media was most useful for:
options: Directly changing government policy through online petitions
Enabling horizontal communication, rapid organization, and international visibility for protest movements
Providing activists with anonymity that made government repression impossible
Replacing the need for charismatic leadership in social movements
questions.explanation: The Arab Spring showed social media's power for decentralized horizontal coordination among activists and for broadcasting events to international audiences despite government media blackouts.

questions.question: A complete, well-rounded digital citizen demonstrates:
options: Maximum time spent online to stay current with digital developments
Skills in critical evaluation, respectful engagement, privacy protection, and responsible information sharing
Expertise in coding and software development
A presence on every major social media platform
questions.explanation: Comprehensive digital citizenship combines media literacy (critical evaluation), digital ethics (respectful, honest interaction), privacy awareness, and responsible contribution to the information environment.
